words: This conversation with NAOMI BOLL PARKER took place at her home in Lewiston,
Idaho on September 1, 1976. The interviewer is SAM SCHRAGER.
NAOMI BOLL PARKER: Now I'm not just sure where they were having this picnic.
She could tell you that, what creek it was on. And for some reason,
Mother said for them to go on she'd come later. And Wallace and I,
we didn t have little brother then. And she said, we were watching
that fire, you know, and she said, "Well, you two stay here, because
I'm going to go just as fast as I can. I've got to get down there
and tell those people they've got to get back." The fire had jumped
the ridge that she could see, you know. And so, she took off. Well,
of course, afterNwhile we thought we d go and find her, too. And I
knew one of the fellows coming back up the trail, and he said, "Listen, I heard something
over there." And, she said, "Wait." and she
called and my gosh it was us kids, Wallace and I. And we had come
down, and I can remember that Wallace was so provoked me because I
was scared to cross this creek. I had to walk a log, see, and nothin'
doin', and I was fussing about walking that log. And so, anyway, when
we got home, of course, Mother began packing things.

speaker: SS
words: What were you kids doing taking off after her? She must have told you

speaker: SS
words: to stay.

speaker: NBP
words: She did, she told us, "Now, you stay because I'm going to go so

speaker: NBP
words: fast you can't keep up with me. And I'm going to be back here." She

speaker: NBP
words: said, "You stay here." But, of course, I suppose I wouldn't listen.

speaker: NBP
words: That I can't remember.

speaker: SS
words: How old were you?

speaker: NBP
words: At that time? Well, I was born in 1906.

speaker: SS
words: And you were four?

speaker: NBP
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: even littler?

speaker: NBP
words: No, Wallace was older. He was born in 1901. But still not

speaker: NBP
words: very old, and she was really in a hurry. And my younger brother was

speaker: NBP
words: born in December and this wasy September, and so you can imagine the

speaker: NBP
words: condition she was in to run down to get these- get the homesteaders

speaker: NBP
words: you know. And so most of the men- our dad was working out of Bovill

speaker: NBP
words: at the time for the Potlatch Lumber Company, see. That's what it was

speaker: NBP
words: then.^ And so, I can remember Mother and my brother was so excited,

speaker: NBP
words: and he was scared he was going to burn, you know. Well, if you've never

speaker: NBP
words: the roar of a terrible forest fire, it's worse than any thunder, I

speaker: NBP
words: think. Two or three times in my life now that I have seen the sky all

speaker: NBP
words: red - well - the horse ranch burned, we thought it was going to

speaker: NBP
words: come- nothing stopping it from coming to take the town, and hot ashes

speaker: NBP
words: were falling. When Slabtown burned all the ashes were falling. I can

speaker: NBP
words: remember. You know those things make an awful impression on a child.

speaker: NBP
words: There s things^you don't remember, but something like that you remem

speaker: NBP
words: ber.

speaker: SS
words: What did she do when she got back to the house? Did she pack?

speaker: NBP
words: She started packing things. And she told my brother, "Now, never mind,"

speaker: NBP
words: she said, "You go out and dig a hole in the garden. I'm going to put

speaker: NBP
words: my silverware and things in there so they won't burn." Because it just

speaker: NBP
words: looked like the fire was coming. You just looked out here and just in

speaker: NBP
words: the distance here was this great big roar and red. Hot ashes falling.

speaker: NBP
words: And she was packing her things. And she had mostly sterling silver set

speaker: NBP
words: that she buried out there. And .: there were over four or five hun

speaker: NBP
words: dred firefighters, but whether they were all concentrated there, I don't

speaker: NBP
words: know, but anyway, I know there were an awful lot of them down there at

speaker: NBP
words: the river. And they came with packhorses for us to ride out, you know.

speaker: NBP
words: And, like I say, Mr. Morrison, took his cow and they lost everything

speaker: NBP
words: that he wasn't able to pack. And she also was expecting a baby in

speaker: NBP
words: December. I don't know how old- he must have been about- I think he

speaker: NBP
words: was maybe three years old or something like that. And she could tell

speaker: NBP
words: you all the places that burned there. I know people by the name of

speaker: NBP
words: Soaper and Kings and whatnot, but I was too small to remember any

speaker: NBP
words: thing like that and the only thing. I remember is just of us and

speaker: NBP
words: how we were packing and I thought I was so busy helping Mother, but

speaker: NBP
words: I was packing my doll clothes and stuff, you know. And she'd throw

speaker: NBP
words: something out, you know, and when she wasn t looking I'd it back

speaker: NBP
words: in. I can remember doing that. And Wallace was so upset that he gave

speaker: NBP
words: her the gun and told her that, "You've got to shoot me." He says,

speaker: NBP
words: "I'm not going to burn!" And, she said, "Oh, give me that." She took

speaker: NBP
words: the gun and she said, "Now get out and dig another hole," she said,

speaker: NBP
words: "I've got some more things to put in there." You know what happened?

speaker: NBP
words: Some firefighters came and they dug up to see what was there. They

speaker: NBP
words: even took her silverware. And she didn't think about it, she had in

speaker: NBP
words: the little tiny cup she had sitting back in the cupboard behind dishes

speaker: NBP
words: and everything, and she had her two diamonds there- rings- they found

speaker: NBP
words: those, too, and took those. And they had even sharpened a couple of

speaker: NBP
words: the sterling silver knives. Didn't take 'em. But, gee, it must have

speaker: NBP
words: been a great shock when she got back.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder why they dug 'em up.

speaker: NBP
words: Well, because they wanted- they're the only ones that were in thereyou

speaker: NBP
words: know, they were fighting fire. Of course, the whole country was

speaker: NBP
words: on fire at that time, because there was fire in Idaho, Washington and

speaker: NBP
words: Montana all through the whole country, you know.

speaker: SS
words: How much was she able to take out of the house?

speaker: NBP
words: Oh, you took nothing. Oh, no, you're riding a horse- just what you

speaker: NBP
words: had on.

speaker: SS
words: So all the stuff that she was getting to gether was to bury?

speaker: NBP
words: Yeah, her keepsakes and things that she wanted.buried, you see. Well,

speaker: NBP
words: of course, she did bury some. But, no, we just took what clothes we

speaker: NBP
words: could have mostly, just with us. And I know they wouldn't let me

speaker: NBP
words: ride on the same horse with her, and I was really upset because I had

speaker: NBP
words: to ride with a man I didn't know, and I didn't like that one bit.

speaker: NBP
words: I can remember fussing about that.

speaker: SS
words: Who did you go out with? Did you go out with other homesteaders?

speaker: NBP
words: Well, yeah. They took us all down to the river, see. That was the

speaker: NBP
words: only safe place they thought, because the fire was coming, and so they

speaker: NBP
words: took us to the river. That's where they had a camp for the firefigh

speaker: NBP
words: ters.

speaker: NBP
words: cabin

speaker: SS
words: Where was the located?

speaker: NBP
words: The cabin?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. Well, now that's why I say, MrsJ\Kha/eu5 can give you all

speaker: SS
words: the-

speaker: SS
words: Was it on Floodwood?

speaker: NBP
words: Oh, no, no, no. Floodwood, you're thinking of Clarkia. This is out

speaker: NBP
words: of Elk River- Elk Creek.

speaker: SS
words: So when they took you down to the river-

speaker: SS
words: Now, whether that was- that's what I don't know. If that was Elk Creek

speaker: SS
words: itself- I can remember near our place there was a small stream, but

speaker: SS
words: down where it was the river. So I really don't know if that was Elk

speaker: SS
words: Creek or not. Mrs. can tell you that.

speaker: SS
words: NBP

speaker: SS
words: When you got down to the river, did they hold you there?

speaker: NBP
words: Well, then they- you see the wind shifted and so then they decided

speaker: NBP
words: that it was safe enough £or us to make our way to Elk River. And so

speaker: NBP
words: then of course, when we got to Elk River why we went on into Bovill.

speaker: NBP
words: And in the meantime my Dad had decided that he would- had rented a lit

speaker: NBP
words: tle house there for us to move into, because all the timber, everything

speaker: NBP
words: was gone from homesteads. Although, our house did not burn like

speaker: NBP
words: so many of the others did. But everybody- I think they just prac

speaker: NBP
words: tically lost everything that they had, you know. And I don't know if

speaker: NBP
words: Mrs. Michaleus might know that, too. How many did go back, but I don't

speaker: NBP
words: think very many of them ever went back because they figured on making

speaker: NBP
words: money on the timber. The company was logging and they were going to

speaker: NBP
words: make- to be able to improve on it and so forth, see.

speaker: SS
words: That was your father's plan?

speaker: NBP
words: Yes. And then the rest of these fellows, too. Morrison and Kings,

speaker: NBP
words: all of them figured on having good stake come in from the timber for

speaker: NBP
words: them. Well, the timber was all burned. But she could give you bet

speaker: NBP
words: ter details than anyone I know. As far as I know, she's the only one.

speaker: SS
words: She was an adult at the time?

speaker: NBP
words: Yes. Yes, she was, you see. Now, Wallace, he had such a horror of it

speaker: NBP
words: that he s blotted out- he doesn't remember. I have asked him if he

speaker: NBP
words: remebered giving Mother the gun and telling her- and he said, no.

speaker: NBP
words: He said, "I just don't," He said, "I just know, that all my life I've

speaker: NBP
words: had such a horror of fire." And I said, "Well, I think it's because

speaker: NBP
words: we were." You know. And again, he was just helping- he was going to

speaker: NBP
words: high school at time, and he was greasing a donkey or something,

speaker: NBP
words: loading, you know, and there was again a forest fire. And, golly,

speaker: NBP
words: those fellows worked like everything. He was just a kid and fought

speaker: NBP
words: to save the camp. Of course, that was down on Potlatch on Little Bear

speaker: NBP
words: Creek., and they used the water out of the stream there. When sparks

speaker: NBP
words: would set a fire, they'd put it out. So they saved the building.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember living or staying in tents or anything during that

speaker: SS
words: period of time? I think I was chatting with Wallace once and he men

speaker: SS
words: tioned to me that on your way out at one point- now was that set up

speaker: SS
words: at Elk River or Bovill?

speaker: NBP
words: I think that was set up in Elk River.

speaker: SS
words: It was a place for people who were coming in from the back country?

speaker: SS
words: NBP Yes, I know that we had to stay down there at the river. And I can

speaker: SS
words: remember seeing this big fire and all these men, and I thought, "Why

speaker: SS
words: have they got a fire when we run from a fire?" But, of course, what

speaker: SS
words: they had was a bonfire, see, and they were cooking coffee, I suppose.

speaker: SS
words: Makeshift deal, you know. I do know, we had lunch there. I remem

speaker: SS
words: ber that.

speaker: SS
words: You know Axel Anderson remebers, when I talked to him, he remembers

speaker: SS
words: being at your place, fighting the fire.

speaker: NBP
words: Oh, is that right?

speaker: SS
words: Oh, yes, he talked to me specifically about your home, and seeing the

speaker: SS
words: family leaving.

speaker: NBP
words: Well, you see, that part I don't remember when we really left.

speaker: NBP
words: But I remember Mother working like mad, you know, just as fast as she

speaker: NBP
words: could. And she had these boxes, see, and my brother had to take them

speaker: NBP
words: out and put them in the garden and bury them. Cover 'em up, so the

speaker: NBP
words: fire wouldn't- We had quite a garden spot. I remember that. And

speaker: NBP
words: she just figured even if the house went, this stuff in the ground should

speaker: NBP
words: be all right, see.

speaker: SS
words: So these fellows dug it up.

speaker: NBP
words: Most of the things they saw, you see, they dug up, to see what was there,

speaker: SS
words: They didn't take anything?

speaker: NBP
words: Well, yeah, diamonds were gone.

speaker: SS
words: Her diamonds were gone?

speaker: NBP
words: Her rings were gone, yeah. And what else, I don't remember.

speaker: NBP
words: But the silverware was- such as sharpening the sterling silver knives.

speaker: NBP
words: Do you think they used your cabin as headquarters?

speaker: NBP
words: Yes, uh-huh, they did.

speaker: SS
words: To fight the fire?

speaker: NBP
words: Uh-huh. After they, well, I don't know- the fire came right up to the

speaker: NBP
words: edge- see, like there was quite a draw with water in, but, of course,

speaker: NBP
words: you know when a fire is rolling, jumping from one place to the other,

speaker: NBP
words: it doesn't even touch the ground for quite a while. And I know that

speaker: NBP
words: those sparks and all, I can remember that , that if they hit you they

speaker: NBP
words: burned. And that happened, too in Bovill when Camp 8 burned.

speaker: SS
words: Was that the 1914 fire off of Bealls Butte?

speaker: NBP
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: You were in Bovill at that time?

speaker: NBP
words: Yes. And so, like I say-

speaker: SS
words: Were you in Bovill? In the town at the time that happened, or were

speaker: SS
words: you at Slabtown?

speaker: SS
words: No, we were on our own place. Let's see, we moved just out at the edge

speaker: SS
words: of town, I think it was 1912, Dad built the house. And this friend

speaker: SS
words: came up to our place and told Mother that they had the train down there

speaker: SS
words: waiting to take the people out of Bovill, see. And they told about

speaker: SS
words: one fellow that was on the train and they asked him where's your wife

speaker: SS
words: and babY? "Oh, my gosh," he said, "I forgot!" He said, "I don't know

speaker: SS
words: where they are!" And there was such crazy things would happen. And

speaker: SS
words: I can remember ; on that train. They had flatcars. And someone

speaker: SS
words: had- out of Camp 8 there, had saved their cookstove. I can see that

speaker: SS
words: cookstove to this day! And anyway, this friend, May 1 and

speaker: SS
words: she told Mother, she said, "Now you pack some clothes and come, because,"

speaker: SS
words: she said, "there's no way. That everything's going to go." Well, our

speaker: SS
words: house, our place, would be the back of it would be facing what was

speaker: SS
words: Camp 8, see, and all that timber and all- although it had been loggedthere

speaker: SS
words: was still enough that it's a cinch that it was go ing to take

speaker: SS
words: that, too, you see. And, so, I remember Mother telling me, "Now you

speaker: SS
words: must have been

speaker: SS
words: go out in the yard there," she had washed, it. Monday, because

speaker: SS
words: in those days you always washed on Monday, so I'm assuming it was Mon

speaker: SS
words: day, "and get my clean stockings off of the line." I had nice white

speaker: SS
words: stockings, and I put on a nice clean dress and little brother, Kenny,

speaker: SS
words: and so May says,"I'll take the two kids and you pack just what you can."

speaker: SS
words: And she took a change of clothes for each one of us and Mother was to

speaker: SS
words: come. And, so of course, again, Mother began packing what she wanted

speaker: SS
words: to save and burying it. And do you know the train pulled out without

speaker: SS
words: my mother. When it got close enough, why, they just blew the whistle

speaker: SS
words: and took off. Everyone was supposed to be on there, you see. And I

speaker: SS
words: don't know if she ever got down to the depot or not, but then the wind

speaker: SS
words: changed, see, and it saved Bovill although it took Camp 8. The wind

speaker: SS
words: changed enough that they could backfire enough and save the town again.

speaker: SS
words: So that little town has been threatened and threatened and threatened,

speaker: SS
words: and each time now it's been saved just by the wind change.

speaker: SS
words: So your house didn't burn?

speaker: NBP
words: No, it didn't burn. And so then here we were.down without our mother

speaker: NBP
words: down to Potlatch. And that was quite a calamity. And this May Feathastorahad

speaker: NBP
words: a friend or relative that she stayed with us. Here she wasnot

speaker: NBP
words: married or anything, just a friend, you know, and here she had two

speaker: NBP
words: kids to take care of. Well of course, Mother came down the next day

speaker: NBP
words: to get us.

speaker: SS
words: But you probably worried about what happened to her.

speaker: NBP
words: Oh, were we! Absolutely, you know. And of course, we wouldn't let

speaker: NBP
words: poor May out of our sight! And I know she tried to get us to go out

speaker: NBP
words: side and play and just nothing doing, just had to sit on both of us

speaker: NBP
words: and I was as big a baby as little brother and I wouldn't let her out

speaker: NBP
words: of my sight either, you know!

speaker: SS
words: What was Wallace doing this time?

speaker: NBP
words: Wallace was- I think he was helping fight fire, carrying water or some

speaker: NBP
words: thing like that. They always had to have Waterboys and so forth car

speaker: NBP
words: rying water to the men that were fighting fire. I don't remember just

speaker: NBP
words: what he was doing at that time.

speaker: SS
words: He was in Bovill?

speaker: NBP
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds like it was not easy a life for your mother.

speaker: NBP
words: It wasn't an easy life. It was a very hard life, for a woman that was

speaker: NBP
words: raised in the East and went to a girls' school and never knew anything

speaker: NBP
words: and came out West- Dad did, to make his fortune. And I remember her

speaker: NBP
words: telling she didn't know how to bake, she didn't know how to do hardly

speaker: NBP
words: anything. She knew hofc/to do fancywork and go to musicals and what

speaker: NBP
words: have you, and so forth. And she had to learn to bake bread; and I'm

speaker: NBP
words: telling you, she's one of the best bakers there ever was. No matter

speaker: NBP
words: what she put her mind to, she did. But she certainly worked hard,

speaker: NBP
words: I should say so. After that experience out there on that homesteadand

speaker: NBP
words: then again, when Dad built this place at first- he had the well

speaker: NBP
words: dug, but the water was full of mineral. .Well, had that scum, so you

speaker: NBP
words: couldn't use it. There was a very good spring down below, but then

speaker: NBP
words: we had to haul the water then. And so, we had a place down there where

speaker: NBP
words: we would wash and had an old cookstove and boiled the clothes; you

speaker: NBP
words: know clothes had to be boiled in those days.

speaker: SS
words: This was down by the spring?

speaker: NBP
words: Yeah, we'd go down to the spring and wash. I can remember that, always

speaker: NBP
words: going down. We never went down anyplace that we didn't have a little

speaker: NBP
words: bucket, and I can remember about a five pound lard bucket, you know.

speaker: NBP
words: Always oome back home with that full of water, so you had fresh water.

speaker: NBP
words: Course we had horses at that time, too. Dad was breaking land, you

speaker: NBP
words: know, and he'd always do this, you see; when he'd get home from his

speaker: NBP
words: day's work with the company, then he'd do this on Saturday or Sunday

speaker: NBP
words: or wheneever he didn't work, why then, we planted the garden and all

speaker: NBP
words: that stuff. And so he had a big fifty gallon barrel on a little drag

speaker: NBP
words: with two runners that he had made- my dad made everything- and we'd

speaker: NBP
words: haul the water. That was Wallace's job, you know, to haul that water.

speaker: NBP
words: That was good enough to take a bath and wash and stuff like that, but

speaker: NBP
words: our drinking water we had to carry.

speaker: SS
words: With the horse, he hauled that water.

speaker: NBP
words: Yes, we hauled the water with the horse. Then we got a mule. Big,

speaker: NBP
words: white mule. Oh, she was a beautiful old thing. We loved her, you know,

speaker: NBP
words: And about four or five could sit on her easy, you know. Oh, she was

speaker: NBP
words: one of those great, big things. And we could do anything we wanted to

speaker: NBP
words: with her. I can remember how much fun we had with her.

speaker: SS
words: Your mother- where in the East did she come from?

speaker: NBP
words: Well, she was raised in around , Wisconsin, but she was born in

speaker: NBP
words: Cayvo and her mother died when she was about three years old. And

speaker: NBP
words: so she went to live with this aunt in Wisconsin, who raised her. And

speaker: NBP
words: that s where she met my dad. He came from a large family and.had a

speaker: NBP
words: farm there. And he had a store when they were married, he owned this

speaker: NBP
words: store in :, Wisconsin. Then, of course, oh, gee, the big sto

speaker: NBP
words: ries about getting rich out West! He was the only one of all of 'emone

speaker: NBP
words: other brother, the younger one- went to Canada, and he didn't get

speaker: NBP
words: his fortune, either- and he went farming- and my dad came- Well, of

speaker: NBP
words: course, he came- Palouse was the town that we lived in, see, and in

speaker: NBP
words: fact, that's where I was born, Palouse, after they had moved out. And

speaker: NBP
words: Dad then worked with the company and they were just building the mill

speaker: NBP
words: Potlatch. Well, then the company would lay steel, track, and he

speaker: NBP
words: built- they moved these- well, they called them shacks that

speaker: NBP
words: they built and built up and had a siding there and had a camp. Then

speaker: NBP
words: They'd log there, you see and the logs were all hauled down to Potlatch.

speaker: NBP
words: Then they kept on and kept on. That's why you've got all those like

speaker: NBP
words: Stanford and Avon and all those. Well, so many of those young fellows

speaker: NBP
words: were students out working in the summer, see. "Well, we'll name this

speaker: NBP
words: place, because this is where I'm going to school." And thats Stanford.

speaker: NBP
words: And another one, well, and Harvard. That s they named those; all

speaker: NBP
words: those little towns.

speaker: SS
words: So for your mother, from what you say- and I have the idea that was

speaker: SS
words: common that young women grew up in the Victorian age to be refined-

speaker: NBP
words: Oh, yes, ! No. No, as you said, what good friends the older women

speaker: NBP
words: were to her.

speaker: SS
words: Out here?

speaker: NBP
words: Yeah. At Palouse Kshe had to learn to bake bread and put up fruit and

speaker: NBP
words: things that she had never heard of before, you know, of doing, because,

speaker: NBP
words: like I say, she was brought up to be a lady. Educated to be a lady.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think she welcomed this kind of life? Or do you think she would

speaker: SS
words: have preferred the city refinements?

speaker: NBP
words: No, I think she adjusted to it, because I know she used to take- when

speaker: NBP
words: we were out on the homestead can remember- sheK Wallace and I

speaker: NBP
words: would go bird hunting. And decide we wanted chicken, alright- maybe

speaker: NBP
words: we had a few chickens, I don't remember, but she taught my brother to

speaker: NBP
words: shoot. And she had learned it herself. Although she never did hunt

speaker: NBP
words: deer or elk or anything like the rest of us did, you know, because

speaker: NBP
words: we grew up in it. But she did that, she was pretty good at shooting

speaker: NBP
words: pheasants. That would be the grouse, you know. And fishing; yes, she

speaker: NBP
words: did a little of that, too. But she did it mostly with us kids, see.

speaker: NBP
words: Because, I suppose there was nothing else to do much, so you just pret

speaker: NBP
words: ty near had to make your own life, you know. There was nothing

speaker: NBP
words: else for you to be doing.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think there was much neighboring going on among the homestea

speaker: SS
words: ders out there?

speaker: NBP
words: There was too much isolation, I think. Although that's why they were

speaker: NBP
words: going to get together on this picnic day- have this picnic. And she

speaker: NBP
